coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots/TiO2 composite and application for enhanced photodegradation of rhodamine B
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Tong, Shijie
Zhou, Jin
Ding, Ling
Zhou, Chuang
Liu, Yi
Li, Shiqian
Meng, Juan
Zhu, Shilin
Chatterjee, Sobhan
Liang, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Titanium dioxide-coated carbon quantum dots (CQDs/TiO2)-based composite was prepared by sol-gel hydrolysis and studied as a catalyst for photodegradation of rhodamine B (RhB) in aqueous solution. The photocatalytic activity and stability of the composite was investigated under visible light. The structure, optical and electrochemical properties of as-prepared samples were characterized by means of SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS, FT-IR, BET, PL, UV–vis/DRS and EIS analyses. The results show that the absorption intensity of the CQDs/TiO2 composite is increased compared to that of TiO2. In addition, the absorption wavelength of the composite material is red-shifted to the visible region. When the content of CQDs in composite is 5 % (based on the mass of TiO2), the obtained CQDs/TiO2 exhibits excellent catalytic activity for the degradation of RhB. The degradation rate of CQDs/TiO2 is 85.47 %, which is 5.5 times higher than that of the pure TiO2. The catalytic degradation rate of RhB remained above 80 % even after 4 cycles. The photocatalytic mechanism of RhB by CQDs/TiO2 reveal holes (h +), superoxide anion radical (•O2 –), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hydroxyl radical (•OH) are the main active species in the photocatalytic process. The oxidizing abilities of the four substances are in the order of •O2 – > h + > •OH > H2O2. This work highlights the enhanced photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TiO2 composite for the treatment of organic pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs/TiO2
Composite
Photocatalysis
Rhodamine B degradation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the National Nature Science Foundation of China (51972242), the Open Fund of the Fujian Universities and Colleges Engineering Research Center of Modern Facility Agriculture of Fujian University (G2-KF2002), the Open Fund of the Hubei Provincial Key Laboratory for New Processes of Ironmaking and Steel making (Wuhan University of Science and Technology) of China (KF-20-5), Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training program for College Students in Hubei Province and Wuhan University of Science and Technology (2022), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Chemical Materials and Devices, Ministry of Education, Jianghan University (JDGD202017).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of pure TiO2 (a) and CQDs/TiO2 composite (b); EDS spectra of pure TiO2 (c) and CQDs/TiO2 composite (d).
TEM images of pure TiO2 (a) and CQDs/TiO2 composite (b); HR-TEM images of pure TiO2 (c) and CQDs/TiO2 composite (d).
XRD patterns of CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 with different CQDs content.
FT-IR spectra of CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2.
The XPS spectra of the whole survey (a), C1s (b), O1s (c) and Ti 2p (d) of CQDs/TiO2.
(a)UV-Vis/DRS absorption spectra of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2, (b) (ahν)2 versus hν plot.
Photoluminescence spectra of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 at different amounts of CQDs.
Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 at different amounts of CQDs.
Transient photocurrent responses of CQDs/TiO2 at different amounts of CQDs.
(a) N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms and (b) corresponding pore size distribution curves of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2.
Degradation efficiency (a, b) and kinetic curves (c, d) of RhB by CQDs/TiO2 catalysts at different amounts of CQDs.
Photocatalytic recycling stability tests on CQDs/TiO2 for RhB degradation.
Effect of scavengers on CQDs/TiO2 for RhB degradation.
Schematic illustration of RhB degradation mechanism of CQDs/TiO2.
Cell parameters and crystallite size of the samples.
Basic parameters for TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots/TiO2 composite and application for enhanced photodegradation of rhodamine B

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Universities and Colleges Engineering Research Center of Modern Facility Agriculture of Fujian University, Fujian Polytechnic Normal University, Fuqing 350300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Universities and Colleges Engineering Research Center of Modern Facility Agriculture of Fujian University, Fujian Polytechnic Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian Universities and Colleges Engineering Research Center of Modern Facility Agriculture of Fujian University, Fujian Polytechnic Normal University, Fuqing 350300, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Titanium dioxide-coated carbon quantum dots (CQDs/TiO2)-based composite was prepared by sol-gel hydrolysis and studied as a catalyst for photodegradation of rhodamine B (RhB) in aqueous solution. The photocatalytic activity and stability of the composite was investigated under visible light. The structure, optical and electrochemical properties of as-prepared samples were characterized by means of SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS, FT-IR, BET, PL, UV–vis/DRS and EIS analyses. The results show that the absorption intensity of the CQDs/TiO2 composite is increased compared to that of TiO2. In addition, the absorption wavelength of the composite material is red-shifted to the visible region. When the content of CQDs in composite is 5 % (based on the mass of TiO2), the obtained CQDs/TiO2 exhibits excellent catalytic activity for the degradation of RhB. The degradation rate of CQDs/TiO2 is 85.47 %, which is 5.5 times higher than that of the pure TiO2. The catalytic degradation rate of RhB remained above 80 % even after 4 cycles. The photocatalytic mechanism of RhB by CQDs/TiO2 reveal holes (h +), superoxide anion radical (•O2 –), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hydroxyl radical (•OH) are the main active species in the photocatalytic process. The oxidizing abilities of the four substances are in the order of •O2 – > h + > •OH > H2O2. This work highlights the enhanced photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TiO2 composite for the treatment of organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of science and industrial technology, the occurrence of various contaminants in aquatic environment has increased dramatically around the world [1,2]. Dye pollution is an important branch of environmental pollution. These dyes are usually highly stable and can remain in the environment for long periods. In recent years, the advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) for wastewater treatment based on reactive oxygen species (ROS) has attracted wide attention from researchers [3,4]. Photocatalysis is a green technology with important application prospect in the field of energy and environment [5]. Organic contaminants can be degraded completely into carbon dioxide and water under the irradiation. At the same time, photocatalytic technology represented by titanium dioxide (TiO2) is considered the revolutionary breakthrough in the field of environmental purification. TiO2 is a kind of photocatalyst with a lot of advantages of simple, low cost, easy to operate, features strong oxidation capacity, good chemical stability, low toxicity, and produces no secondary pollution. Especially under light radiation, TiO2 can generate highly active photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and improve the utilization of photogenerated carriers in photocatalytic reactions. So TiO2 is regarded as a promising dye wastewater treatment photocatalyst [6,7].

However, due to the wide band gap of TiO2 (Anatase: 3.2 eV; Rutile: 3.0 eV), only the ultraviolet ray that accounts for 3–5 % of the solar spectrum can be used effectively, which limits its wide applications [8]. In order to effectively shorten the band gap of nanobands and improve the photocatalytic activity, TiO2 can be coupled with semiconductor materials, metal ions, dyes and non-metal elements to form composites [9,10]. It has been reported that the doping of N, C, S, Fe and F elements improved the photocatalytic effect of TiO2 [11–13]. However, the introduction of partially doped heteroatoms and defects promotes the recombination of photogenerated carriers, resulting in unsatisfactory photocatalytic effects. So, in order to improve the properties of TiO2, we can construct the TiO2-based composite using the nanomaterial, such as metal nanoparticles, metal oxide nanoparticles, quantum dots, etc. On one hand, the composite can combine the properties of both nanoparticles and TiO2, and on the other hand, can bring more photocatalytic active sites due to the unique interaction between the two species.

As a new type of environmentally friendly carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted much attention due to their diverse synthesis methods, good biocompatibility, and unique photo-induced electron transfer. In addition, CQDs can be used in various fields, such as electronics, photoluminescence, and electrochemistry [14–16]. CQDs not only possess advantages of quantum dots and carbon materials, but also can avoid the coagulation problem of TiO2 when combined with TiO2 [17]. Therefore, CQDs could be a better modifier in preparing TiO2 without limiting their performance. There are many CQDs/TiO2 composites have been prepared through deposition and hydrothermal method [18,19]. Yu et al. have prepared CQDs/TiO2 composite by loading CQDs on P25 (commercial TiO2) using deposition method that can be used to carried out effectively the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, but the result also shows that CQDs and TiO2 could not be recombined uniformly, which result in low photocatalytic efficiency [18]. Although this efficiency has been improved to some extent and some progresses have been made, the role of CQDs in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants system has not been detailedly researched. Hence, it is necessary to explore the preparation methods and applications of CQDs/TiO2 with excellent photocatalytic properties, and dive into the photocatalytic degradation capability and mechanism for RhB of CQDs/TiO2 through improving experimental scheme and taking advantage of the synergetic effect of CQDs and TiO2.

In this paper, the eco-friendly CQDs were prepared with cheap and recyclable coke powders as raw materials, and then the enhanced photocatalytic CQDs/TiO2 composites were prepared by a sol-gel method. During the preparation process, CQDs were added to the reaction solution at the TiO2 gel stage to make the CQDs and TiO2 combine more uniformly and fully exert the photocatalytic synergetic effect. The CQDs prepared by our lab [18,19] have the most stable size and performance under acid condition, and hydrolysis of tetrabutyl titanate is also need to acidic condition, both of which can be obtained the very good synergetic effect. This preparation method is different from other based-TiO2 composites preparation process. In addition, the step of high temperature calcination is also different from other methods. High temperature calcination can well adjust the crystallinity, crystal type proportion of TiO2 and the dispersion of CQDs. The morphology and structure of the composites were characterized using a variety of techniques. Furthermore, to demonstrate the application potential of the composites in photocatalysis, the photocatalytic activity and stability of the composites were investigated by photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B (RhB). The CQDs/TiO2 composite has potential prospects for application in the photodegradation for treatment of organic wastewater.

Coke powders, industrial grade (99 %), were obtained from Wuhan Iron and Steel Limited Co. of China. Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %) was supplied by Xinyang Chemical Reagent Factory of China. Concentrated nitric acid (HNO3, 68 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), tetrabutyl titanate (C16H36O4Ti), anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH), glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %) were all supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd of China. Rhodamine B (RhB) was supplied by Tianjin North Fine Chemicals Development Co., Ltd of China. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade.

CQDs were prepared by reported chemical oxidation method [20]. 0.1 g of coke powder (particle size less than 0.1 mm) was taken and 16 mL of mixed acid was added. The mixed acid was made up of concentrated sulfuric acid and concentrated nitric acid according to the volume ratio of 3:1. The mixture was fully stirred and reacted for 8 h at 90 ℃. After the band reaction, the supernatant was taken after cooling, centrifugation and filtration. The supernatant was neutralized with 5 mol/L NaOH solution and adjusted to pH= 7. The purified CQDs were dialysed by dialysis bag and freeze-dried. TiO2 was prepared by reported sol-gel method [21].

CQDs/TiO2 composite was prepared by sol-gel method. Various combinations of the calcination and activation temperature, time, and TiO2 to CQDs mass ratio (m TiO2:m CQDs) were used in preparation to obtain the composite with optimized photocatalytic properties. The CQDs/TiO2 composite with excellent photocatalytic activity could be prepared when the mass ratio of TiO2:CQDs, heating reaction temperature, and reaction time were 95:5, 550 °C, and 120 min, respectively. At this time, 10 mL of butyl titanate was slowly dropped into 35 mL of absolute ethanol, and then vigorously stirred for 10 min at room temperature to form a yellow clear mixture A. 4 mL of glacial acetic acid and 10 mL of distilled water were added to another 35 mL of anhydrous ethanol, and was stirred vigorously to obtain the mixture solution B. Subsequently, the solution B was adjusted by dropping a few drops of hydrochloric acid to maintain a desired pH≤ 3. Then, the 0.1 g CQDs solution and solution A was slowly dropped into the solution B at a rate of about 10 mL/min to obtain a light yellow solution, respectively. The pale yellow solution was stirred for 0.5 h and heated in a water bath at 80 °C for 1 h to obtain a pale yellow gel. The gel was filtered and dried to obtain a light yellow powder. Finally, the powder was calcination and activated at 550 ℃ for 120 min. After the reaction was finished, the kettle was naturally cooled to room temperature. The resulting CQDs/TiO2 composites was obtained. The optimization preparation conditions of CQDs/TiO2 composites were listed in Supplementary Materials (see Figs. S1, S2 and S3).

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Nava 400, FEI, Waltham, MA, USA) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100UHR, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV were used to characterize the morphologies of the catalyst, and the Energy dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS, Nava 400, FEI, Waltham, MA, USA) was used for elemental analysis. The structure of catalyst was tested and analyzed by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X pert Pro MPD, Philips, Netherlands), Cu target, Kα (λ = 0.154056 nm, U = 40 kV, I = 50 mA). The oxidation state of the elements in the catalyst was tested and identified by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, 250XI, Thermo Fisher, Waltham, MA, USA). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of the sample was carried out by a Nicolet 6700 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, MA, USA). The optical properties were characterized by a 721 visible light spectrophotometer (Youke Corp., Shanghai, China) and a UV-Vis Diffuse Reflectance Spectrometer (UV-Vis/DRS, CARY 300, Agilent, USA). Further, the N2 adsorption and desorption experiments were used to show the pores of the catalyst and structure, and calculate its specific surface area. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by an Agilent Cary Eclipse PL spectrometer. Electrochemical detection was tested in a standard three-electrode cell System via electro chemical station (CHI 760E, Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation method of photocatalytic activity and stability
ce:section.ce:para: In the photocatalytic degradation experiment, 0.2 g of the catalyst was utterly dispersed in 200 mL of 10 mg/L RhB solution, and the mixture was magnetically stirred for 40 min in a dark environment at 25 ℃. The pH value of RhB solution was 3 before degradation reaction. After that, the 300 W xenon lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter simulated visible light for degradation experiments. In a certain interval, a pipette was used to get 4 mL suspension sample every 20 mins. The suspension was centrifuged and separated immediately at a speed of 6,000 r/min for obtaining the supernatant.

The absorbance of RhB supernatant was measured by spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 554 nm. Record the absorbance value of each sample taken. In the control experiment, only one variable was changed at a time. In the free radical detection experiment, tert-butanol was added to the solution. For repeated experiments of photocatalytic degradation, the photocatalyst was collected by centrifuging after the degradation experiment and washed with deionized water and ethanol three times before the next test run. After that, the catalyst was dispersed in a system with the same parameters. All experiments were repeated four times, the average value was taken.

The degradation rate of RhB can be calculated by the following formula (1) [22]:
(1)
D =
(
c
0
−
c
t
)
c
0
* 100 % =
(
A
0
−
A
t
)
A
0
* 100 %
Where D is the degradation rate of RhB; C
0 is the concentration of RhB before reaction, mg/mL; C
t is the concentration of RhB at time t, mg/mL; A
0 is the absorbance of RhB before reaction. A
t is the absorbance of RhB at time t.

In order to study the photocatalytic mechanism and identify the main intermediate active species in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB, the capture experiments of hydroxyl radical (•OH), hole (h+), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and superoxide anion radical (•O2 -) were carried out. By investigating the change of photocatalytic activity before and after adding the trapping agent, the main active species were identified. Isopropanol (IPA) is a •OH trapping agent. Ammonium oxalate (AO) is an h+ trapping agent. Catalase (CAT) has a rapid decomposition effect on H2O2. P-benzoquinone (BQ) is a •O2 - trapping agent. Before the dark reaction in the photocatalytic experiment, free radical scavengers calculated in proportion were added to each experiment respectively. Catalytic degradation of substrates at 200 mL, 10 mg/mL RhB. By adding 0.02 g of IPA, AO, CAT, and BQ to the photocatalytic reaction, the changes in RhB concentration with time were measured under the same conditions, respectively [23,24]. The photocatalytic degradation experiments were carried out under visible light and UV light, respectively.

The morphology, structure and elemental composition of the materials were determined by SEM and EDS. The SEM images of the as-prepared pure TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite are shown in Fig. 1. Although the morphology and structure of the pure TiO2 (Fig. 1(a)) and CQDs/TiO2 composite (Fig. 1(b)) are basically similar, there are differences between them. The pure TiO2 agglomerate into larger particles. The size of the CQDs/TiO2 is slightly smaller than that of TiO2, which might result from increasing of crystal defects of composite. And the loading of CQDs on the TiO2 can increase specific surface area of CQDs/TiO2, thus increasing the dispersion of composites in solution. Fig. 1(c) and Fig. 1(d) are EDS spectra of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2. The C, O and Ti contents in CQDs/TiO2 composites are 4.64, 46.86 and 48.53 wt %, respectively. The O and Ti contents in TiO2 are 47.24 and 52.24 wt %, respectively. The introduction of CQDs mainly reduces the Ti content, which indicates that the Ti atoms in the TiO2 are replaced successfully by CQDs.

TEM is used to further detect the microscopic crystal structure of catalyst. The TEM and HR-TEM images of the as-prepared pure TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composites were shown in Fig. 2. Fig. 2(a) and Fig. 2(b) are TEM images of pure TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2, respectively. Compared with pure TiO2, the CQDs/TiO2 crystal is covered with CQDs, making the crystal closer to sphere. Fig. 2(c) and Fig. 2(d) are HR-TEM images of pure TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2, respectively. The lattice fringes with the spacing of 0.351 nm corresponding to the crystal plane 101 of TiO2, and the spacing of 0.235 nm corresponding to the crystal plane 001 of TiO2, can be observed obviously in both images[24]. Besides the lattice fringes of TiO2, the images of CQDs also can be observed in Fig. 2(d). It is similar to the CQDs prepared by our laboratory [25]. These results indicate that the as-prepared composites contain TiO2 crystals and CQDs, and part of CQDs exist in the TiO2 crystals.

Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composites. As shown in the figure, the XRD patterns of CQDs/TiO2 composites in the presence of different content CQDs are basically the same as the 2θ position of the characteristic peak of pure TiO2. The characteristic peaks of anatase TiO2 appear at 25.28°, 37.80°, 48.05°, 53.89°, 55.06°, 62.69°, 70.31° and 75.03° (JCPDS no. 21–1272). Using the Debye-Scherrer equation and the reflection at 25.28°, the average crystallite size of CQDs/TiO2 was estimated and the results was given in Table 1. The obtained results revealed that the addition of CQDs into the TiO2 remarkably hindered crystalline growth. Additionally, the (101) plane was applied to CQDs/TiO2 to find out lattice values as well as cell volume (Tetragonal phase, a = b ‡ c). The lattice parameter and the cell volume of the CQDs/TiO2 were also reported in Table 1. The lattice constants of TiO2 in the nanocomposites are slightly lower than those of pure TiO2. Both of these values could be matched with those presented in JCPDS no. 21–1272 standard for TiO2 [26].

However, compared with the diffraction peaks of pure TiO2, the intensity of the diffraction peaks of the CQDs/TiO2 composites decreases, and the half-peak width becomes wider. This may due to the fact that, in the process of high temperature calcination, the part of CQDs enter the crystal lattice gap of TiO2 and disturb the crystallization process of TiO2, which reduces the crystallinity of TiO2 and produces lattice defects [12,21]. In addition, the wide peaks appear between 20° and 25° and the peak intensity also increases with the increase of CQDs content. These wide peaks correspond to the diffraction peaks of CQDs [24]. However, if one looks at the data carefully, there is a decrease in intensity of 10 % CQDs. This may be because excessive CQDs load on the surface of TiO2 can cause particles to aggregate, thus leading to a slight peak intensity decrease of composites. The loading of CQDs on TiO2 can reduce the particle size and increase the specific surface area of CQDs/TiO2, thereby improving the catalytic activity of RhB degradation. A small amount of CQDs can bring about crystal defects during the formation of TiO2 crystal, which is beneficial to the separation of photogenerated electron and holes in the process of photocatalysis. However, the excessive CQDs is not conducive to the formation of anatase and rutile crystal structures to a certain extent.

The FT-IR spectra were used to study the chemical composition and functional groups of the materials. The FT-IR spectra of the pure CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 were compared in Fig. 4. The pure CQDs peaks located at 2,077; 1,109; 678; and 600 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibration of the asymmetric carbon-carbon triple bond of alkynes (-CC), SP3 hybrid alkyl ether bond, out-of-plane bending vibration of cis-disubstituted alkene C-H, and out-of-plane bending vibration of terminal alkyne C-H, respectively. The peaks located at 3,417; 1,630 cm−1 and 1,392 cm−1 in the CQDs/TiO2 are attributed to the O-H tensile vibration, O-H bending vibration, and tensile vibration of CO skeleton in carboxylate, respectively. As can be seen, the band of pure TiO2 observed around 600 cm−1 belongs to Ti-O-Ti bonds. Compared with pure TiO2, a wide absorption band of CQDs/TiO2 composites was shifted below 1,000 cm−1, indicating that Ti-O-C bond was formed in composites [25,27]. The results suggest that there might be a large number of hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs, while the hydroxyl groups in TiO2 are relatively little. The hydroxyl groups increase on the surface of CQDs/TiO2 after TiO2 forming complexes with CQDs. The hydroxyl groups have extremely important effect on the photocatalytic activity of composites. The hydroxyl could interact with holes to generate hydroxyl radicals with strong oxidizing properties, thus promoting the photocatalytic oxidation of TiO2 [28,29].

XPS was used to investigate the chemical composition and crystal structure as presented in Fig. 5 [30]. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 5(a)) proves the presence of C, O and Ti elements. The main peak at 284.8 eV can be ascribed to the C-C and CC chemical bonds of CQDs in Fig. 5(b). The two relatively weak peaks at 286.02 and 288.86 eV correspond to the CO and C-O, respectively, which indicate the presence of CQDs in the composites [31]. The spectrum of O1s showed energy peaks at the positions of 529.73, 531.69 and 532.68 eV (Fig. 5(c)), corresponding to the Ti-O, C-O and O-H, respectively, which proved that the formation of Ti-O-C between CQDs and TiO2 [18]. In Fig. 5(d), the two characteristic binding energies of Ti 2 P3/2 and Ti 2 P1/2 appear at 458.43 eV and 464.15 eV, respectively. According to the XPS spectrum of pure TiO2 in the literature [32]，Compared with pure TiO2, the position of two peaks shifted slightly due to the existence of Ti-O-C in the CQDs/TiO2 composite, which further proves that chemical bond recombination exists between the TiO2 and CQDs. For XPS data, for example, FWHM, relative percentage, peak position, the results are listed in Table 2.

In order to study the utilization of different wavelengths of light by the photocatalytic materials, the UV-Vis/DRS spectra of the materials were detected [33]. The UV-Vis/DRS spectra of pure TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 are shown in Fig. 6(a). It can be seen from the figure that the pure TiO2 photocatalyst has no obvious absorption in the range of 400–800 nm, which is in line with the characteristic light absorption of TiO2. However, the CQDs/TiO2 catalyst shows obvious absorption in the visible light range. The absorption spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 red-shifts compared to that of TiO2, which is calculated by the following formula (2) [21]:
(2)
Eg =
1240 eV / nm
λ g
Where Eg is the band gap width of material; λg is the band wavelength, nm.

As shown in Fig. 6(b), the band gap energy of CQDs/TiO2 composite decreased to 2.98 eV compared with that (3.13 eV) of pure TiO2. With the decrease in forbidden band gap, a red shift occurred in the light absorption sideband of CQDs/TiO2, thus expanding the utilization range of UV–vis light. The absorption intensity of CQDs/TiO2 increased in the ultraviolet region. The results show that CQDs can improve the optical activity of TiO2.

Fig. 7 shows the PL spectra of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2. As shown Fig. 7, all samples exhibited fluorescence emission peaks at 475 nm. TiO2 showed the strongest luminescence, whereas 5 % CQDs/TiO2 presented the weakest luminous intensity. The order of luminescence intensity is as follows: TiO2 > 10 % CQDs/TiO2 > 7.5 % CQDs/TiO2 > 2.5 % CQDs/TiO2 > 5 % CQDs/TiO2. When the photocatalyst is stimulated by light, a strong fluorescence intensity results in the smooth combination of photogenerated electrons with holes, and vice versa, resulting in effective separation. Surface modification of TiO2 with suitable amounts of CQDs was proven effective in separating photogenic carriers, thus showing strong photocatalytic performance [34,35].

EIS measurements were performed in the presence of a 2.5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] as a redox probe at a potential of 0.2 V. The EIS of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 prepared under different conditions was investigated using Nyquist plots ( Fig. 8). In all EIS plots, the arc radius at high frequency provides details regarding charge transfer resistance. Thus, a small arc radius implies a fast reaction. The resistance during charge transfer can be analyzed by EIS plots [36]. Comparing the arc radii for all samples, the introduction of CQDs onto the TiO2 surface led to a decrease in charge transfer resistance, suggesting that separation and transportation of photogenerated electrons and holes proceeded rapidly. The size of the arc radius of the 5 % CQDs/TiO2 species is evidently smaller than that of other samples, consistent with PL data. Hence, the separation and transfer of electron hole increased, and the photocatalytic performance expectedly improved [37].

Fig. 9 shows the transient photocurrent response curves of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts under visible light irradiation. As shown Fig. 9, the CQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts generate significant photocurrent signal when they are radiated by visible light, which indicates that the CQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts are sensitive to visible light. The photocurrent density of 5 % CQDs/TiO2 is about 3 times of the pure TiO2. Because CQDs can capture photogenerated electrons generated by photoexcitation of TiO2 and inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in the semiconductor, thus prolonging the life of photogenerated electrons and enhancing the signal of photogenerated current [37]. However, excessive CQDs loading on the surface of TiO2 can form aggregation, which lead to the decrease of photocurrent density [38]. The experimental results further prove that appropriate amounts of CQDs can improve effectively the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers in photocatalyst.

N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 are shown in Fig. 10. As can be seen from Fig. 10(a), the TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 both belong to type-Ⅳisotherm and H3 hysteretic loop according to IUPAC [39], which are the characteristic isotherm curve of mesoporous materials and indicate the existence of meso-pores. It is related to the slit holes of aggregation of lamellar particles [40].

The adsorption quantity of CQDs/TiO2 is much higher in comparison with that of TiO2 in all relative pressure ranges, which indicates that the specific surface area of CQDs/TiO2 increased because of the introduction of CQDs. The CQDs anchored on the surface of TiO2 can increase the surface roughness of TiO2 and provide more active sites for photocatalytic reaction. The TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 possess mesoporous properties in the range of 2–25 nm, as displayed in Fig. 10(b). In addition, there are two obvious pore distribution peaks, the smaller peak is about 1.7 nm, and the larger peak is about 15 nm, which testifies that these materials possess mesoporous properties. In the range of 1.7–5.0 nm pore size distributions, the pore volume decreases slightly, which may be due to the decrease of CQDs loading [41].

The calculated BET specific surface area, pore volume and pore size data of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 are listed in Table 3. The specific surface area and pore volume of pure TiO2 are 20.1 m2/g and 0.08 cm3/g, respectively. In comparison with the pure TiO2, the specific surface area and pore volume of CQDs/TiO2 increased to 55.7 m2/g and 0.182 cm3/g, respectively. The average pore diameter varies from 5.9 nm to 13.1 nm. These results show rather mesoporous nature of prepared materials. The increase of specific surface area and pore volume is more beneficial for the diffusion and adsorption of pollutants because it can increase the reaction space and provide effective paths for movement of reactants and products, leading to improved catalytic activity [35].

The degradation results of RhB by visible light of CQDs/TiO2 composites with different CQDs weight percentage content are shown in Fig. 11. The catalytic degradation efficiency of CQDs/TiO2 on RhB increase firstly and then decrease with the increasing of CQDs content in CQDs/TiO2 composites (Fig. 11(a)). It can be observed that the visible light catalytic efficiency of pure TiO2 only is 15.56 %. When the CQDs content is 5 %, the degradation efficiency of CQDs/TiO2 composites is highest, up to 85.47 % (Fig. 11(b)), which is 5.5 times as high as that of pure TiO2. When the CQDs content is lower than 5 %, the visible light catalytic efficiency of composites increases gradually with the increasing of CQDs content, indicating that the introduction of appropriate amount CQDs have positive influence on improving photocatalytic performance of composites [42]. A small amount of CQDs can bring about crystal defects during the formation of TiO2 crystal, which is beneficial to the separation of photogenerated electron and holes in the process of photocatalysis. However, further increasing the amount of CQDs will bring about a significant decrease of photocatalytic activity. The excessive CQDs is not conducive to the formation of anatase and rutile crystal structures to a certain extent. It may be owning to the reduced catalytic active sites and increased charge carriers recombination rate and the decreasing of the separation efficiency of photo-induced electron-hole pairs caused by excessive CQDs loaded on the surface of TiO2 [17,21,43].

Therefore, the optimal CQDs content is 5 % in the CQDs/TiO2 composite, and the corresponding product present the best photocatalytic activity. Fig. 11(c). and Fig. 11(d). depict the corresponding kinetic curves fitted with a pseudo-first-order kinetic Eq. (3) [44]:
(3)
ln
c
0
c
t
= kt
Where k is the kinetic rate constant of photocatalytic degradation; t is the time of photocatalytic reaction, min; c
0 is the RhB concentration before reaction, mg/mL; c
t is the RhB equilibrium concentration at time t, mg/mL; respectively.

It is observed that the introduction of CQDs significantly prompts reaction kinetic process. The 5 % CQDs/TiO2 composite displays the highest photocatalytic degradation capability, and the corresponding rate constant of degradation (k = 0.0157 min−1) is 14.2 times than that of pure TiO2 (k = 0.0011 min−1).

Lifetime of a photocatalyst is a significant factor in determining the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the photocatalyst, as considerable cost reduction can be achieved if the photocatalyst can be used for many cycles and for longer period. In order to evaluate whether the composite catalyst can be used in a stable cycle, four cycles of degradation of RhB were carried out. It can be seen from Fig. 12, Results of four cycles show that 85.47 % of degradation of RhB is degraded in first cycle and the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of CQDs/TiO2 catalyst for RhB is still up to 82.44 % after repeated use four times. Slightly decreased photocatalytic efficiency in four cycles might be due to the loss of some active sites of the catalyst surface by localization of produced byproducts over it, causing reduction of incident photons collection efficiency of the catalyst [45,46]. Consequently, photogenerated electron-hole pairs reduced, which reduced the hydroxyl radicals, and resultantly degradation efficiency of the catalyst was slightly decreased [47].
Thus indicating the CQDs/TiO2 catalyst prepared by sol-gel method has a good stability for RhB degradation and can still maintain high efficiency photocatalytic performance after repeated use.

Fig. 13 demonstrates a comparsion the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 and TiO2 photocatalyst in the presence of four different scavengers for degradation of RhB in visible light. The results show that the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 always is higher than that of pure TiO2 with or without scavenger.

The TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 catalyst activity decreased significantly in the presence of BQ. The catalyst activity only somewhat decreased in the presence of the CAT. As can be seen from Fig.13, under visible and ultraviolet light irradiation, the •OH, h+ and •O2 - all play important roles in the process of RhB degradation. The catalyst activity vary with the type of active species used for RhB degradation. The four active species effect on catalyst activity is in order of •O2 - > h + > •OH > H2O2.

Fig. 14 demonstrates the RhB photocatalytic degradation pathway and degradation mechanism of CQDs/TiO2. Under visible light irradition, the catalyst can be excited to generate conduction band electron (e -) and valence band hole (h +) through electron transition. The active e - in the conduction band reacts with O2 to generate •O2 -, and a series of further reactions generate H2O2, •OH and OH-, etc., while the h + in the valence band reacts with H2O to generate •OH, and the final oxidation and degradation of RhB is completed by the degradation of active •OH and O2. In addition, the photogenerated e- and h+ are easily recombined, reducing the photocatalytic efficiency. The reaction equation is shown in Eqs. (4)-(12) [21,48]:
(4) CQDs/TiO2 + hv → h + +e −
(5) O2 + e - → •O2 –
(6) •O2 – + 2 H+ +e−→ H2O2
(7) H2O2 → HO2 −+H+
(8) O2 + 2 H+ + e−→ H2O2
(9) H2O2 + e−→ OH- + •OH
(10) H2O + h + → •OH + H+
(11) RhB + •OH + O2 → CO2 +H2O + RO x
(12) H+ + e−→hv

In the photocatalysis process, in addition to the above photocatalytic reactions, there are the following unique reactions at the surface of CQDs/TiO2 catalyst:
(13)
T
i
4 +
+ e
−
→ T
i
3 +
(14)
∙
O
2
−
+ 2
h
+
→
1
2
oxygen vacancy

The Ti4+ can react with photogenerated e - to produce Ti3+ (Eq. (13)) [49,50] and inhibit the recombination of e - and h +, which facilitate the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, according to the previous X-ray energy spectrum analysis, CQDs can replace part of Ti4+ in CQDs/TiO2 and capture e -, which also inhibits effectively the recombination of e - and h + and promote the photocatalytic reaction. So among above these steps, the reaction of •O2 - and h + is the crucial step (Eq. (14)) [48]. The •O2 - and h + play a decisive role in the photocatalysis process, which is consistent with the conclusion of the previous scavenger experiment.

The eco-friendly CQDs were prepared with cheap and recyclable coke powder as carbon source, and the CQDs/TiO2 composites with enhanced photocatalytic performance were prepared by CQDs loading. The catalytic degradation efficiency of CQDs/TiO2 on RhB increase firstly and then decrease with the increasing of CQDs content in CQDs/TiO2 composites. When the CQDs content is 5 %, the surface area of CQDs/TiO2 reached 55.7 m2.g−1, and the degradation efficiency of CQDs/TiO2 composite is highest, up to 85.47 %, which is 5.5 times as high as that of pure TiO2. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of CQDs/TiO2 catalyst for RhB remained above 82.44 % after repeated use four times, showing strong photochemical stability. The photocatalytic mechanism showed that •OH, h +, H2O2 and •O2 - were the main active species in the photocatalytic process. The catalyst activity vary with the type of active species used for RhB degradation. The order of the influence of the four active species on the catalyst activity is •O2 - > h+ > •OH > H2O2. Therefore, CQDs/TiO2, as an efficient and green photocatalyst, has great potential applications in the photocatalytic treatment of organic pollutants.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface modification of TiO2 with polydopamine and its effect on photocatalytic degradation mechanism
